question: What is biometric data?
answer:
Biometric data refers broadly to information derived from automatic measurements of a person's unique biological characteristics that can be used to identify them. Common examples cited across jurisdictions include fingerprints, voiceprints, eye retinas, and irises. The core idea is that the data must be capable of identifying a specific individual — it is not enough that biological characteristics are measured; those measurements must serve an identification purpose.

Illinois takes a somewhat different approach under its Biometric Information Privacy Act, distinguishing between "biometric identifiers" (the raw physical markers, such as retina or iris scans, fingerprints, voiceprints, or hand/face geometry scans) and "biometric information" (any information, regardless of how captured or stored, that is based on a biometric identifier and used to identify someone) [BIPA 740 ILCS 14/10]. Illinois also has an extensive list of explicit carve-outs, including writing samples, photographs, X-rays, MRI and other medical imaging, genetic material regulated under the Genetic Information Privacy Act, and data collected in health care settings under HIPAA [BIPA 740 ILCS 14/10].

Several other state laws define "biometric data" more concisely as data generated by automatic measurements of biological characteristics used to identify a specific individual. Connecticut, Kentucky, Rhode Island, and Oregon all follow this general pattern [CTDPA Sec. 1(3); KCDPA Sec. 1(3); RIDTPPA Sec. (3); OCPA 646A.570(3)]. Maryland's definition focuses specifically on characteristics that can uniquely authenticate a consumer's identity [modpa-full-text 14–4601(D)]. All of these laws agree that ordinary photographs, audio or video recordings, and data derived from them are not biometric data — unless that data was specifically generated or used to identify an individual.

Oregon goes a step further by explicitly excluding facial mapping or facial geometry from the definition unless it was generated or used for the purpose of identifying a specific consumer [OCPA 646A.570(3)(b)(D)]. Kentucky and Illinois also carve out health care data collected under HIPAA, while Illinois adds the most detailed exclusions of any jurisdiction, including donated organs, cadaveric transplant materials, and medical imaging [BIPA 740 ILCS 14/10; KCDPA Sec. 1(3)].
